passages.explanation: When Jesus finishes instructing his twelve disciples, he goes on to teach and preach in their cities. John, in prison, hears about the works of Christ and sends his disciples to ask: are you the one who is coming, or should we look for another? Jesus answers: go and tell John the things you hear and see — the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor have good news preached to them. Blessed is he who finds no occasion for stumbling in me. Even John the Baptist has doubts in a dark prison cell. Jesus doesn't rebuke the question — he answers with evidence.

As John's disciples leave, Jesus speaks to the crowds about John. What did you go out into the wilderness to see — a reed shaken by the wind? A man in soft clothing? Those are in kings' houses. A prophet? Yes, more than a prophet. This is the one about whom it's written: I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way. Among those born of women, no one has risen greater than John the Baptiser, yet the least in the Kingdom of Heaven is greater than he. From the days of John until now, the Kingdom of Heaven suffers violence, and the violent take it by force. All the prophets and the law prophesied until John. If you're willing to receive it, this is Elijah who is to come. He who has ears, let him hear. John is the greatest of the old order — but the new order that Jesus brings surpasses even the greatest.

To what shall I compare this generation? It's like children sitting in the marketplaces calling to their companions: we played the flute for you and you didn't dance. We mourned and you didn't lament. John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say he has a demon. The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say: look, a gluttonous man and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners. Wisdom is justified by her children. John was too austere. Jesus is too social. The real problem isn't the messenger's style — it's the audience's unwillingness to respond.

Jesus begins to denounce the cities where most of his mighty works were done, because they didn't repent. Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! If the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes. It will be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgement than for you. And you, Capernaum — will you be exalted to heaven? You'll go down to Hades. If the mighty works done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until today. Greater privilege means greater accountability. The cities that saw the most miracles receive the harshest warning.

Jesus says: I thank you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you hid these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to infants. Yes, Father, for so it was well-pleasing in your sight. All things have been delivered to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. Come to me, all you who labour and are heavily burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. My yoke is easy, and my burden is light. After pronouncing woes on the unrepentant, Jesus turns and offers the most tender invitation in all the gospels. The same Jesus who judges also welcomes.
